title: ELECTROCONVULSIVE THERAPY (ECT) Course for a 45-Year-Old Woman with SEVERE TREATMENT-RESISTANT Major Depressive Disorder (Melancholic Features, Psychomotor Retardation, Food/Fluid Refusal, Active Suicidal Ideation) After Failing Multiple Antidepressant Trials: Confirming DECISIONAL CAPACITY in Severe Depression and Witnessing INFORMED CONSENT, Pre-Procedure PREPARATION (NPO + Dental Check + Removing Dentures + Holding Lithium and Benzodiazepines/Anticonvulsants — but NOT Routine Pre-Op Anxiolytics), Coordinating the Intra-Procedure Team (Psychiatrist + Anesthesia + ECT RN — Methohexital + SUCCINYLCHOLINE + ATROPINE/Glycopyrrolate + Bite Block + EEG-Verified Brief Generalized Seizure), POST-Procedure Side-Lying Recovery and Re-Orientation with Expected Transient Confusion/Memory Effects, Course Management Over 6-12 Sessions 3 Times/Week, and Family Education that Dispels Stigma
subtitle: ELECTROCONVULSIVE THERAPY (ECT) is the most effective acute treatment for SEVERE depression, often the right choice when illness is dangerous and time matters: treatment-resistant major depression after adequate trials, severe depression with active SUICIDALITY or FOOD/FLUID REFUSAL or CATATONIA or PSYCHOTIC features, severe MANIA, NEUROLEPTIC MALIGNANT SYNDROME (rapid resolution), and selected catatonia or treatment-resistant schizophrenia with affective features. It can be lifesaving — response rates approach 70-80% in severe depression — and safety is comparable to general anesthesia for other minor procedures (mortality ~1 in 75,000 treatments). There are NO ABSOLUTE contraindications; RELATIVE precautions include recent MI/unstable cardiac disease, severe HTN, recent stroke or intracranial mass with raised ICP, severe pulmonary disease, severe osteoporosis, pheochromocytoma, and retinal detachment. Mechanism — under brief general anesthesia and muscle relaxation, scalp electrodes (BILATERAL or RIGHT-UNILATERAL) deliver a brief electrical stimulus that induces a generalized cerebral SEIZURE of about 30-60 seconds on EEG; the motor convulsion is largely suppressed by the muscle relaxant so the patient lies still apart from minimal movements. The mood effect is thought to involve broad neurochemical, neuroendocrine, and neuroplastic changes — not fully understood. CAPACITY in severe depression is a key teaching point: depression does NOT automatically negate decisional capacity (links case-116/132/142). Capacity is decision-specific and presumed; the standard 4-element test (understands, appreciates, reasons, communicates a stable choice) is applied to THIS treatment. Even active suicidality does not preclude capacity for ECT consent; the nurse confirms voluntariness, screens for coercion, and supports the patient in deliberation. If capacity is LACKING, a surrogate consents under substituted judgment (links case-132); emergency ECT is permissible only when life is in immediate danger (e.g., refractory catatonia, refusal of all food/fluids causing imminent harm) and other criteria are met per state law/ethics. PRE-PROCEDURE preparation (the RN role) is procedural-safety work: confirm IDENTITY and procedure with two identifiers; verify CONSENT and capacity; medical clearance with H&P, ECG, basic labs and a DENTAL evaluation (loose teeth, removable appliances); NPO after midnight for general anesthesia; remove dentures, jewelry, hairpins; have hair clean (no oils/sprays for electrode contact); empty the bladder; patent IV; baseline vitals; explain the day to the patient and family; allow family at bedside if the patient wishes. MEDICATION decisions before ECT are high-yield: HOLD LITHIUM (raises risk of prolonged seizures, post-ECT delirium, and neurotoxicity), HOLD BENZODIAZEPINES and HOLD ANTICONVULSANTS (they RAISE the seizure threshold and blunt the ECT seizure — coordinate the taper with the prescriber over preceding days); antidepressants and most antipsychotics are usually continued (specific exceptions per protocol); a routine pre-op ANXIOLYTIC is NOT given because it would suppress the seizure. INTRA-PROCEDURE in the ECT suite — psychiatrist + anesthesiologist + ECT-trained RN — uses pre-oxygenation; a brief-acting general anesthetic (commonly METHOHEXITAL — propofol can blunt seizure quality; etomidate is an option); SUCCINYLCHOLINE for short-acting muscle relaxation; ATROPINE or GLYCOPYRROLATE to blunt vagal bradycardia and reduce secretions; a BITE BLOCK to prevent dental and tongue injury; continuous ECG/EEG/SpO2/BP monitoring; the brief generalized seizure is verified on EEG (target ≥ ~25-30 seconds for an adequate treatment); the typical cardiovascular pattern is an initial parasympathetic dip (bradycardia/hypotension) followed by a sympathetic surge (tachycardia/hypertension), managed by anesthesia. A 'cuff' tourniquet on one limb before succinylcholine isolates motor seizure for observation. POST-PROCEDURE — transfer to recovery in the SIDE-LYING position (aspiration risk while sedated), continuous oximetry and frequent vital signs until awake, oxygen as needed, GENTLE re-orientation with simple words and a calm voice (expect TRANSIENT CONFUSION and short-term memory effects for 30-60 minutes), treat HEADACHE/MUSCLE ACHES with acetaminophen and NAUSEA with antiemetics per orders, assess fall risk and ambulate only with assistance, resume diet and routine medications when alert and able to swallow safely. EDUCATE on what to expect across the COURSE: typically 6-12 sessions, 3 times a week (e.g., Monday/Wednesday/Friday), with mood improvement often after 3-4 sessions; common side effects are headache, muscle aches, nausea, and TRANSIENT CONFUSION/short-term memory effects (retrograde and anterograde) that usually improve within weeks; long-term/persistent memory complaints can occur and are discussed openly; maintenance ECT (less frequent) may follow to prevent relapse. Always dispel stigma: modern ECT does not look like the 'shock therapy' shown in old films — the patient is asleep, the convulsion is minimal, brain imaging shows no structural damage, and the treatment is one of the most evidence-supported in psychiatry. PERTINENT MISTAKES TO AVOID: assuming severe depression equals incapacity and bypassing the patient; failing to remove dentures or check for loose teeth; giving a pre-op anxiolytic or failing to hold lithium/benzos/anticonvulsants per protocol; positioning the patient supine in recovery before fully awake; over-explaining or quizzing during early post-ictal confusion; treating each session in isolation rather than coordinating the course; describing ECT as 'shock therapy' or 'brain frying' in front of the patient/family. NURSING ROLE: confirm capacity and witness informed consent; coordinate pre-procedure preparation (medical clearance, NPO, dental, removal of appliances, medication holds, baseline vitals); collaborate with anesthesia and psychiatry in the suite (bite block, monitoring, seizure verification); deliver safe post-procedure recovery (side-lying, airway, vitals, re-orientation, comfort, fall prevention); manage the course over 6-12 sessions with consistent education and family partnership; document objectively at every step.

patient.name: M. Pavlik
patient.code: Full code; inpatient psychiatric admission for severe treatment-resistant major depression with active suicidal ideation and food/fluid refusal; goal = safe coordinated ECT course — confirm capacity and witness informed consent, complete pre-procedure preparation (medical clearance, NPO, dental, medication holds, removal of appliances), collaborate intra-procedure with psychiatry + anesthesia, deliver side-lying post-procedure recovery with gentle re-orientation, manage the 6-12-session course, partner with family, and document at every step
patient.admitDate: Inpatient psychiatric unit, day 4 of admission, with SEVERE major depressive disorder, melancholic features, marked PSYCHOMOTOR RETARDATION, persistent active SUICIDAL IDEATION with a plan, and food/fluid refusal (~14 lb weight loss in 6 weeks). Adequate trials of sertraline, then venlafaxine with augmentation (mirtazapine, then aripiprazole) have failed over the past year. Psychiatry recommends ECT as the appropriate next step given severity, refractoriness, suicidality, and food refusal. Formal capacity assessment by psychiatry today confirms decision-making capacity is intact (understand/appreciate/reason/communicate a stable choice) — depression alone does NOT negate capacity. Informed consent for an acute ECT course (6-12 sessions, 3x/week) is signed in the family meeting with husband present; the RN witnesses consent and addresses remaining questions. Today is the morning of the first ECT treatment.
patient.room: Inpatient psychiatry; ECT suite available; interdisciplinary team (psychiatrist, anesthesiologist, ECT-trained RN, primary care/medical-clearance MD, dental consult, pharmacy, social work, family liaison); husband present

vitals.temp: 36.8°C; alert + cooperative + tearful; psychiatry confirms decisional CAPACITY (understand/appreciate/reason/communicate a stable choice — severe depression alone does NOT negate capacity); informed consent for an acute ECT course signed with husband present; the RN witnesses and confirms understanding/voluntariness, addresses questions, and screens for coercion

36.8°C; NPO after midnight verified; identity + procedure verified with two identifiers; medical clearance ECG/labs reviewed; DENTAL evaluation done — no loose teeth; dentures + jewelry + hairpins REMOVED; hair clean (no sprays/oils); bladder emptied; patent IV; LITHIUM and BENZODIAZEPINES + ANTICONVULSANTS HELD per protocol; antidepressants continued per provider; routine pre-op anxiolytic NOT given (would suppress the seizure)

36.8°C; ECT suite; psychiatrist + anesthesiologist + ECT RN; pre-oxygenation; methohexital induction + SUCCINYLCHOLINE muscle relaxation + ATROPINE for vagal blockade; BITE BLOCK placed; continuous ECG/EEG/SpO2/BP; right-unilateral electrode placement chosen for cognitive preservation; brief generalized SEIZURE ~38 seconds on EEG (adequate); cardiovascular pattern handled by anesthesia

36.9°C; positioned SIDE-LYING to protect the airway from aspiration; continuous oximetry + q5-15 min vitals until awake; gentle re-orientation with simple words and a calm voice (expected TRANSIENT confusion ~30-60 min and short-term memory effects); headache treated with acetaminophen, nausea with ondansetron per order; airway intact; able to swallow before resuming diet; assisted ambulation only (fall risk from disorientation)

36.8°C; sessions Mon/Wed/Fri for an acute course of 6-12 treatments; meaningful mood improvement after sessions 3-4 — increased eye contact, more spontaneous speech, improved appetite, no current suicidal intent; transient short-term memory effects discussed openly with patient and husband; lithium and benzos remain held during the course per protocol; antidepressants continued

36.7°C; course completed with sustained remission; transition plan includes maintenance antidepressant, possible maintenance ECT (less frequent) if relapse-risk warrants, outpatient psychiatry + psychotherapy, safety planning, family education on warning signs, and follow-up; husband is informed and engaged; documentation complete; stigma actively addressed throughout

labs.n: PRESENTATION + indications for ECT + capacity confirmation + informed consent
labs.v: 45 y/o woman M. Pavlik with SEVERE treatment-resistant major depressive disorder (melancholic features, marked psychomotor retardation, persistent active suicidal ideation with a plan, food/fluid refusal with ~14 lb weight loss) after adequate trials of sertraline, venlafaxine, and augmentation (mirtazapine then aripiprazole). ECT is indicated for treatment-resistant depression, especially with suicidality, food/fluid refusal, catatonia, or psychotic features; for severe mania; for NMS; and for selected treatment-resistant schizophrenia. Response in severe depression is ~70-80%; safety is comparable to general anesthesia for minor procedures. There are NO ABSOLUTE contraindications — RELATIVE precautions include recent MI/unstable cardiac disease, severe HTN, recent stroke or raised ICP, severe pulmonary disease, severe osteoporosis, pheochromocytoma, and retinal detachment. CAPACITY in severe depression is the high-yield ethical point: depression alone does NOT negate decisional capacity (links [[case-116]]/[[case-132]]/[[case-142]]). Psychiatry confirms capacity by the 4-element test (understands/appreciates/reasons/communicates a stable choice); active suicidality does not preclude capacity for ECT consent. The psychiatrist obtains consent; the NURSE WITNESSES the signature and confirms understanding and voluntariness, addresses remaining questions, and screens for coercion. If capacity were absent, surrogate consent applies per substituted judgment (links [[case-132]]); emergency ECT is reserved for immediate life-threat (refractory catatonia, refusal of all food/fluids causing imminent harm). Husband is involved and supportive; family/cultural context explored.
labs.r: ECT indications: treatment-resistant depression (#1), severe depression w/ suicidality / food refusal / catatonia / psychotic features, severe mania, NMS, selected schizophrenia. Response ~70-80% in severe depression; safety like minor general anesthesia. NO ABSOLUTE contraindications; RELATIVE: recent MI/unstable cardiac, severe HTN, recent stroke/↑ICP, severe pulm, severe osteoporosis, pheo, retinal detachment. CAPACITY in severe depression is PRESUMED + decision-specific (4 elements); active suicidality ≠ incapacity. Psychiatrist obtains consent; NURSE WITNESSES + confirms understanding/voluntariness + addresses questions + screens for coercion. RN role: capacity + consent + family.

labs.n: PRE-procedure preparation + medication decisions (HOLD lithium / benzos / anticonvulsants; NO routine anxiolytic)
labs.v: Pre-procedure preparation is procedural-safety work coordinated by the RN. Verify IDENTITY and procedure with two identifiers and the consent; review medical clearance — H&P, ECG (cardiac risk), basic labs, and a DENTAL evaluation (loose teeth/removable appliances). NPO AFTER MIDNIGHT for general anesthesia (~6-8 hours; sips of water for essential meds per protocol). Have the patient REMOVE dentures, jewelry, hairpins, and contact lenses; ensure HAIR is clean (no oils/sprays that block electrode contact); empty the bladder; ensure a patent IV; baseline vital signs; explain the day in plain language to the patient and family. MEDICATION DECISIONS are high-yield. HOLD LITHIUM around ECT (it potentiates the seizure, prolongs post-ECT delirium, and adds neurotoxicity risk; coordinate stop-and-resume with the prescriber). HOLD BENZODIAZEPINES and HOLD ANTICONVULSANTS for the period defined by the protocol (they RAISE the seizure threshold and blunt the ECT seizure); a flumazenil reversal is considered only in special cases. ANTIDEPRESSANTS and most ANTIPSYCHOTICS are usually continued (specific exceptions per protocol — e.g., bupropion can lower seizure threshold and is sometimes held). Routine pre-op ANXIOLYTIC is NOT given because it would suppress the seizure; address anxiety with therapeutic communication, the family at bedside, and a clear walk-through of the day. Confirm a final time-out with the team before transport to the ECT suite.
labs.r: Pre-procedure: two identifiers + consent + clearance (H&P/ECG/labs/DENTAL); NPO after midnight; REMOVE dentures/jewelry/hairpins/contacts; clean hair (no oils/sprays); empty bladder; patent IV; baseline vitals; plain-language explanation. MEDS: HOLD LITHIUM (prolonged seizure/delirium/neurotoxicity), HOLD BENZOS + ANTICONVULSANTS (raise threshold → blunt seizure); antidepressants + most antipsychotics usually continued (bupropion sometimes held). NO routine pre-op ANXIOLYTIC (suppresses seizure); manage anxiety with TC + family + walk-through. RN role: prep + meds + time-out.

labs.n: INTRA-procedure team + POST-procedure recovery + side effects + family education
labs.v: INTRA-PROCEDURE in the ECT suite uses pre-OXYGENATION; brief general anesthesia (commonly METHOHEXITAL — propofol can blunt seizure quality; etomidate is an option); SUCCINYLCHOLINE for short-acting muscle relaxation; ATROPINE or glycopyrrolate to blunt vagal bradycardia and reduce secretions; a BITE BLOCK to protect the teeth and tongue; continuous ECG/EEG/SpO2/BP monitoring; the brief generalized SEIZURE is verified on EEG (typical target ~25-60 seconds for an adequate treatment). A 'cuff' tourniquet on one limb before succinylcholine isolates the motor seizure for observation. The cardiovascular pattern is typically an initial PARASYMPATHETIC dip (transient bradycardia/hypotension) followed by a SYMPATHETIC surge (tachycardia/hypertension), managed by anesthesia. RIGHT-UNILATERAL placement is often chosen for cognitive preservation; BILATERAL placement may be used when faster efficacy is needed. POST-PROCEDURE — transfer to recovery in the SIDE-LYING position (sedated airway is at risk of aspiration), oxygen as needed, continuous oximetry and q5-15 min vitals until awake, GENTLE re-orientation with simple words and a calm voice (expect TRANSIENT confusion ~30-60 minutes and short-term memory effects); treat HEADACHE/MUSCLE ACHES with acetaminophen and NAUSEA with antiemetics per orders; assess fall risk and ambulate only with assistance; resume diet and routine medications when alert and able to swallow safely. SIDE EFFECTS to expect across the course: headache, muscle aches, nausea, transient confusion, and short-term MEMORY effects (RETROGRADE for events around the procedure and ANTEROGRADE for new learning during the course) that usually improve within weeks; persistent memory complaints can occur and are discussed openly. COURSE: typically 6-12 sessions, 3x/week (e.g., Mon/Wed/Fri) with meaningful mood improvement often after 3-4 sessions; maintenance ECT (less frequent) may follow to prevent relapse. FAMILY education partners with the patient throughout and DISPELS STIGMA: modern ECT is performed under general anesthesia with minimal motor convulsion, is one of the most evidence-supported treatments in psychiatry, and has no demonstrated structural brain injury on imaging — it is NOT the 'shock therapy' of old films.
labs.r: INTRA: pre-O2; METHOHEXITAL (propofol blunts seizures; etomidate option) + SUCCINYLCHOLINE + ATROPINE/glycopyrrolate; BITE BLOCK; continuous ECG/EEG/SpO2/BP; seizure ~25-60s on EEG; cuff tourniquet isolates motor seizure. CV pattern: PARASYMPATHETIC dip (brady/hypoT) → SYMPATHETIC surge (tachy/HTN) — managed by anesthesia. RIGHT-UNILATERAL preserves cognition; BILATERAL faster efficacy. POST: SIDE-LYING (aspiration), O2 + q5-15 min vitals + oximetry, GENTLE re-orientation (transient confusion 30-60 min + short-term memory effects); acetaminophen for HA/myalgia, antiemetics for nausea; assisted ambulation (fall risk); resume diet/meds when alert + swallow intact. COURSE: 6-12 sessions 3x/wk; improvement after 3-4; maintenance ECT possible. EDUCATE: side effects + memory + stigma (modern ECT ≠ old 'shock therapy'; evidence-supported). RN role: intra-team + recovery + side effects + family.

labs.n: Pertinent NEGATIVES + protective factors + course documentation + transition
labs.v: PERTINENT NEGATIVES (correct practice): the nurse did NOT assume severe depression equals incapacity and bypass the patient (psychiatry confirmed capacity; the patient signed consent with full understanding); did NOT skip the dental evaluation or fail to remove dentures/jewelry/hairpins (airway and dental injury risk); did NOT give a routine pre-op ANXIOLYTIC or continue LITHIUM/BENZODIAZEPINES/ANTICONVULSANTS through the morning of ECT against protocol (they would suppress or distort the seizure or add neurotoxicity); did NOT position the still-sedated patient supine in recovery (aspiration risk — sidelying instead); did NOT over-explain or quiz during early post-ictal confusion (gentle reorientation only); did NOT describe ECT to the patient/family as 'shock therapy' or 'frying the brain' (modern ECT is performed under general anesthesia and is one of the most evidence-supported treatments in psychiatry — stigma is actively addressed). PROTECTIVE FACTORS: documented capacity assessment and witnessed consent; comprehensive preprocedure preparation (clearance + NPO + dental + appliances removed + correct medication holds + baseline vitals); coordinated intra-procedure care (psychiatry + anesthesia + ECT RN with bite block + monitoring + EEG-verified adequate seizure + cardiovascular management); safe recovery (sidelying + airway + vitals + gentle reorientation + comfort + fall prevention); consistent EDUCATION across the course (side effects + memory + course schedule + stigma); family partnership; objective DOCUMENTATION at every step (consent, capacity, vitals, anesthesia, seizure duration, recovery, response, side effects); and a clear TRANSITION PLAN to maintenance therapy + outpatient psychiatry + psychotherapy + safety planning. EXPECTED outcome: a safe ECT course with meaningful mood improvement after sessions 3-4 and remission by course completion; transient confusion and short-term memory effects that resolve over weeks; sustained recovery with maintenance pharmacotherapy and/or maintenance ECT and active relapse-prevention follow-up.
labs.r: Negatives confirm correct practice (didn't equate severe depression w/ incapacity, didn't skip dental/remove appliances, didn't give routine anxiolytic or fail to hold lithium/benzos/anticonvulsants, didn't position supine in recovery, didn't over-quiz post-ictal, didn't label modern ECT 'shock therapy'). Protective: documented capacity + witnessed consent + comprehensive prep + coordinated intra-procedure care + safe sidelying recovery w/ gentle reorientation + consistent education across course + family partnership + objective documentation + clear transition (maintenance pharm + outpatient psych/therapy + safety planning + maintenance ECT consideration). RN role: coordinate the course safely and humanely.

nursesNote: Inpatient psychiatry, day 4 — 45 y/o woman w/ SEVERE treatment-resistant MDD (melancholic features, marked psychomotor retardation, persistent active SI w/ plan, food/fluid refusal, ~14 lb weight loss) after failed adequate trials (sertraline → venlafaxine + mirtazapine then aripiprazole augmentation). ECT recommended as the appropriate next step given severity + refractoriness + suicidality + food refusal. Psychiatry CAPACITY assessment today: intact (understand/appreciate/reason/communicate stable choice — severe depression alone does NOT negate capacity); active SI does NOT preclude capacity. Informed consent for an acute ECT course (6-12 sessions, 3x/wk) signed with husband present; RN WITNESSED + confirmed understanding/voluntariness + addressed questions + screened for coercion. PRE-PROCEDURE (morning of first treatment): two-identifier ID + consent verified; medical clearance ECG/labs reviewed; DENTAL exam — no loose teeth; dentures + jewelry + hairpins REMOVED; hair clean (no sprays/oils); bladder emptied; patent IV; baseline vitals normal; LITHIUM and BENZOS + ANTICONVULSANTS HELD per protocol (raise threshold/blunt seizure or add neurotoxicity); antidepressants continued; NO routine pre-op anxiolytic (would suppress seizure); time-out completed; husband at bedside. INTRA-PROCEDURE in ECT suite — psychiatrist + anesthesiologist + ECT RN: pre-O2 → methohexital induction + SUCCINYLCHOLINE muscle relaxation + ATROPINE for vagal blockade; BITE BLOCK placed; continuous ECG/EEG/SpO2/BP; right-unilateral electrode placement chosen for cognitive preservation; brief generalized SEIZURE ~38 sec on EEG (adequate); typical parasympathetic dip → sympathetic surge managed by anesthesia. POST-PROCEDURE: transferred to recovery SIDE-LYING (aspiration risk); continuous oximetry + q5-15 min vitals + supplemental O2 prn; gentle re-orientation w/ simple words + calm voice (transient confusion ~30-60 min, short-term memory effects expected); HA treated w/ acetaminophen + nausea w/ ondansetron per order; assisted ambulation only (fall risk); resumed diet + meds once alert + swallowing intact. COURSE — Mon/Wed/Fri x 6-12 sessions: meaningful mood improvement after sessions 3-4 (better eye contact + more spontaneous speech + appetite returning + no current SI), transient short-term memory effects discussed openly w/ pt + husband; lithium + benzos remain held; antidepressants continued. TRANSITION: maintenance antidepressant + outpatient psychiatry + psychotherapy + safety planning + possible MAINTENANCE ECT (less frequent) for relapse prevention; family educated on warning signs; follow-up scheduled. NEGATIVES (correct practice): did NOT assume severe depression = incapacity, did NOT skip dental/remove-appliances, did NOT give routine pre-op anxiolytic or continue lithium/benzos/anticonvulsants against protocol, did NOT position supine in recovery, did NOT over-quiz during post-ictal confusion, did NOT label modern ECT 'shock therapy' (stigma actively addressed). Documentation complete at every step (consent + capacity + vitals + anesthesia + seizure duration + recovery + response + side effects).
providerOrders: DIAGNOSIS: severe treatment-resistant major depressive disorder with melancholic features, active suicidal ideation, and food/fluid refusal; capacity confirmed by psychiatry; informed consent witnessed by the RN

TREATMENT: acute electroconvulsive therapy course — 6-12 sessions, 3x/week (Mon/Wed/Fri) per psychiatry; right-unilateral electrode placement initially for cognitive preservation; reassess response and side effects after each session

MEDICATION HOLDS around ECT: HOLD lithium per protocol (prolonged seizure/delirium/neurotoxicity); HOLD benzodiazepines and anticonvulsants per protocol (raise seizure threshold and blunt the seizure); continue antidepressants and most antipsychotics per protocol; do NOT give a routine pre-op anxiolytic on the morning of ECT

PRE-PROCEDURE PREPARATION: verify identity and procedure with two identifiers; confirm consent and capacity documentation; complete medical clearance (H&P, ECG, labs) and dental evaluation; remove dentures, jewelry, hairpins, and contact lenses; clean hair (no oils or sprays); empty bladder; patent IV; baseline vital signs; NPO after midnight; final team time-out

INTRA-PROCEDURE TEAM (ECT suite): pre-oxygenation; methohexital induction (or alternative per anesthesia); SUCCINYLCHOLINE for muscle relaxation; ATROPINE or glycopyrrolate for vagal blockade and secretions; BITE BLOCK in place; continuous ECG, EEG, SpO2, and BP monitoring; verify an adequate brief generalized seizure on EEG (typically ~25-60 seconds); a 'cuff' tourniquet on one limb to isolate motor seizure for observation; anesthesia manages the parasympathetic dip then sympathetic surge

POST-PROCEDURE RECOVERY: transfer to PACU/recovery in the SIDE-LYING position (aspiration risk); supplemental O2 as needed; continuous oximetry and frequent vital signs (q5-15 minutes until awake); gentle re-orientation with simple words and a calm voice; treat headache and muscle aches with acetaminophen and nausea with ondansetron per order; assess fall risk and ambulate with assistance only; resume diet and routine medications when alert and able to swallow safely

MONITORING ACROSS THE COURSE: response (mood, suicidal ideation, sleep, appetite, function), side effects (headache, myalgia, nausea, transient confusion, short-term memory effects — retrograde and anterograde), and any prolonged seizure (>2 minutes) or post-ECT delirium

EDUCATION (patient + family): expected course (6-12 sessions; improvement often after 3-4), common side effects, memory effects (typically transient; discuss openly), what ECT looks like under modern anesthesia (DISPEL STIGMA — not the 'shock therapy' of old films), and how to support the patient between sessions

TRANSITION/RELAPSE PREVENTION: maintenance antidepressant per psychiatry; consider maintenance ECT (less frequent) if relapse-risk warrants; outpatient psychiatry and psychotherapy follow-up; safety planning; involve family

DOCUMENTATION: capacity assessment, witnessed informed consent, pre-procedure verification + holds, intra-procedure participants and seizure duration, post-procedure vitals/recovery/side effects, response across the course, and the transition plan

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What matters at the start of this ECT course?
steps.inst: Select ALL findings/considerations that are clinically significant.

opts.text: Severe, treatment-resistant major depression with active suicidality and food/fluid refusal — ECT is indicated as the appropriate next step
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — this is exactly the high-yield NCLEX indication set for ECT. RN role: recognize indication.

opts.text: Severe depression does NOT automatically negate decisional capacity; psychiatry confirmed intact capacity by the 4-element test and the RN WITNESSES the informed consent
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the capacity-in-depression rule and the nurse's witness role. RN role: capacity + consent.

opts.text: Pre-procedure preparation must include medical clearance, dental evaluation, removal of dentures/jewelry/hairpins, NPO after midnight, an empty bladder, and a clean scalp (no oils/sprays)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — procedural-safety steps that prevent aspiration, dental injury, and electrode-contact failure. RN role: prep checklist.

opts.text: Medication decisions are protocol-driven: HOLD lithium, HOLD benzodiazepines and anticonvulsants, continue most antidepressants/antipsychotics, and do NOT give a routine pre-op anxiolytic
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — high-yield medication holds that determine seizure quality and safety. RN role: coordinate holds.

opts.text: In the ECT suite the team uses pre-oxygenation, methohexital, succinylcholine, atropine/glycopyrrolate, a bite block, and continuous ECG/EEG/SpO2/BP monitoring with verification of an adequate brief generalized seizure on EEG
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the standard intra-procedure team and monitoring. RN role: collaborate intra-procedure.

opts.text: Post-procedure recovery is in the SIDE-LYING position with gentle re-orientation; transient confusion and short-term memory effects (retrograde + anterograde) are expected and improve over weeks
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — recovery position + expected side effects. RN role: safe recovery + reorientation.

opts.text: Assume severe depression equals incapacity and have the husband sign the consent for her without her involvement
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Depression alone does NOT negate capacity; capacity is decision-specific and presumed, and the patient must consent if capable.

opts.text: Give a routine pre-procedure anxiolytic on the morning of ECT to help her relax and continue her lithium and clonazepam through the morning dose
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Anxiolytics raise the seizure threshold and suppress the ECT seizure; lithium and benzodiazepines/anticonvulsants are held per protocol around ECT.

opts.text: ECT is indicated for treatment-resistant depression and for severe depression with suicidality, food/fluid refusal, catatonia, or psychotic features; for severe mania; for NMS; and for selected treatment-resistant schizophrenia
opts.rat: CORRECT — indication set. Foundation for choosing ECT.

opts.text: There are NO absolute contraindications to ECT; relative precautions include recent MI/unstable cardiac disease, severe HTN, recent stroke or raised ICP, severe pulmonary disease, severe osteoporosis, pheochromocytoma, and retinal detachment
opts.rat: CORRECT — contraindications principle. Foundation for risk stratification.

opts.text: Capacity in severe depression is presumed and decision-specific; active suicidality does not by itself preclude capacity for ECT consent
opts.rat: CORRECT — capacity principle. Foundation for honoring autonomy.

opts.text: Lithium and benzodiazepines/anticonvulsants are held per protocol around ECT; routine pre-op anxiolytics are NOT given because they suppress the seizure
opts.rat: CORRECT — medication principle. Foundation for seizure quality and safety.

opts.text: Intra-procedure team includes psychiatrist + anesthesiologist + ECT RN with pre-oxygenation, methohexital, succinylcholine, atropine/glycopyrrolate, bite block, and continuous ECG/EEG/SpO2/BP monitoring with seizure verification on EEG
opts.rat: CORRECT — intra-procedure principle. Foundation for safe technique.

opts.text: Post-procedure recovery uses the side-lying position with gentle re-orientation; common side effects include headache, myalgia, nausea, and transient confusion/short-term memory effects that usually improve within weeks
opts.rat: CORRECT — recovery and side-effect principles. Foundation for safe care.

opts.text: Modern ECT causes lasting structural brain damage and is essentially the same as the 'shock therapy' shown in old films
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Modern ECT is performed under general anesthesia with minimal motor convulsion; imaging shows no demonstrated structural injury; outcomes data are strongly in favor of efficacy and safety.

opts.text: The patient should be placed supine immediately after ECT to make airway management easier in recovery
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. The side-lying position protects the still-sedated airway from aspiration; supine increases aspiration risk.

opts.text: Confirm decisional capacity and witness informed consent (involve the family with the patient's consent)
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. Capacity and consent are the ethical and legal foundation of the entire course. RN role: capacity + consent.

opts.text: Complete pre-procedure preparation — medical clearance, dental check, NPO, removal of dentures/jewelry/hairpins, clean scalp, baseline vitals, patent IV — and confirm medication holds (lithium, benzodiazepines, anticonvulsants) with NO routine pre-op anxiolytic
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Correct prep prevents aspiration/dental injury and ensures an adequate seizure. RN role: prep + holds.

opts.text: Collaborate with the intra-procedure team — pre-oxygenation, methohexital, succinylcholine, atropine, bite block, ECG/EEG/SpO2/BP — and verify an adequate seizure
opts.rat: #3 — Safe technique in the suite. RN role: collaborate intra-procedure.

opts.text: Deliver safe post-procedure recovery in the side-lying position with airway, vitals, gentle re-orientation, comfort, and fall prevention
opts.rat: #4 — Protects the airway and supports the disoriented patient. RN role: recovery.

opts.text: Provide consistent education across the course (side effects, memory, schedule, stigma), partner with family, plan the transition (maintenance therapy + outpatient follow-up + safety plan), and document objectively
opts.rat: #5 — Sustains gains and prevents relapse. RN role: course education + transition + documentation.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH action, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this ECT course.

opts.text: Have psychiatry confirm capacity with the 4-element test; the RN witnesses informed consent and confirms understanding/voluntariness
opts.rat: Honors autonomy and meets the legal-ethical foundation. RN role: capacity + consent witness.

opts.text: NPO after midnight, dental evaluation, remove dentures/jewelry/hairpins/contacts, clean scalp (no oils/sprays), empty bladder, patent IV, baseline vitals, and a team time-out
opts.rat: Core procedural-safety steps. RN role: prep.

opts.text: HOLD lithium and HOLD benzodiazepines and anticonvulsants around ECT per protocol; continue antidepressants and most antipsychotics; do NOT give a routine pre-op anxiolytic
opts.rat: Determines safe seizure quality. RN role: med holds.

opts.text: Use pre-oxygenation, methohexital, succinylcholine, atropine/glycopyrrolate, a bite block, and continuous ECG/EEG/SpO2/BP monitoring with EEG-verified seizure
opts.rat: Standard intra-procedure care. RN role: intra-procedure team.

opts.text: Position the patient SIDE-LYING in recovery with continuous oximetry and frequent vitals; gentle re-orientation; treat headache/myalgia/nausea per order; assisted ambulation only
opts.rat: Protects airway and supports the disoriented patient. RN role: recovery.

opts.text: Educate patient and family across the course (side effects, memory, schedule), dispel stigma about ECT, partner with the family, and plan transition (maintenance + outpatient + safety planning)
opts.rat: Sustains the course and prevents relapse. RN role: education + transition.

opts.text: Assume severe depression equals incapacity and have the husband sign consent for her without her involvement
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — depression alone does not negate capacity; the patient must consent if capable.

opts.text: Give a routine pre-op anxiolytic and continue her lithium and clonazepam through the morning of ECT
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — anxiolytics suppress the seizure; lithium and benzodiazepines/anticonvulsants are held per protocol.

opts.text: Place the patient supine immediately after ECT and quiz her on dates and details to test her memory while she is still confused
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — supine risks aspiration; quizzing during post-ictal confusion is unkind and unhelpful. Use side-lying and gentle re-orientation.

opts.text: Describe modern ECT to the patient and family as 'shock therapy' or 'frying the brain'
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — stigmatizing language is harmful; describe modern ECT accurately and dispel stigma.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of the nurse's actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Confirm capacity and witness informed consent, involve the husband with the patient's consent, and answer questions
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. The ethical and legal foundation comes first. RN role: capacity + consent.

opts.text: Complete pre-procedure preparation (medical clearance + dental + NPO + remove appliances + clean scalp + baseline vitals + patent IV) and confirm medication holds; do NOT give a routine pre-op anxiolytic
opts.rat: #2 — Second. Correct preparation enables a safe, adequate treatment. RN role: prep + holds.

opts.text: Collaborate with the intra-procedure team — pre-oxygenation, methohexital, succinylcholine, atropine, bite block, continuous ECG/EEG/SpO2/BP — and verify an adequate seizure
opts.rat: #3 — Third. Safe technique in the suite. RN role: intra-procedure team.

opts.text: Deliver post-procedure recovery in the side-lying position with airway, vitals, gentle re-orientation, comfort, and fall prevention
opts.rat: #4 — Fourth. Protects airway and supports the disoriented patient. RN role: recovery.

opts.text: Provide consistent education across the course, partner with family, dispel stigma, and plan the transition (maintenance therapy + outpatient + safety planning) with documentation throughout
opts.rat: #5 — Closing/ongoing. Sustains gains and prevents relapse. RN role: education + transition + documentation.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each action/outcome, classify as APPROPRIATE or INAPPROPRIATE.

opts.text: Psychiatry confirmed decisional capacity; the RN witnessed informed consent and addressed questions; the husband was included with the patient's permission
opts.rat: Honors autonomy and meets the ethical-legal foundation. Model practice.

opts.text: Pre-procedure preparation was complete (medical clearance + dental + NPO + appliances removed + clean scalp + baseline vitals + patent IV) with the correct medication holds (lithium + benzodiazepines + anticonvulsants) and NO routine pre-op anxiolytic
opts.rat: Procedural-safety bundle done correctly. Correct trajectory.

opts.text: Intra-procedure: pre-oxygenation, methohexital + succinylcholine + atropine, bite block, continuous ECG/EEG/SpO2/BP, EEG-verified brief generalized seizure ~38 seconds, parasympathetic dip then sympathetic surge managed by anesthesia
opts.rat: Safe, monitored, EEG-verified adequate treatment. Correct trajectory.

opts.text: Post-procedure: side-lying recovery, frequent vitals and oximetry, gentle re-orientation, acetaminophen for headache and ondansetron for nausea, assisted ambulation; education across the course dispelled stigma and prepared the family
opts.rat: Protects airway, supports disorientation, and sustains the course. Correct trajectory.

opts.text: By session 4, mood improves, eye contact and speech increase, appetite returns, and active suicidal ideation resolves; the course is completed with remission and a transition plan
opts.rat: Expected response to an evidence-supported treatment, followed by relapse-prevention planning. Ideal outcome.

opts.text: Alternative — the team assumed severe depression equals incapacity and had the husband sign consent for her without her involvement
opts.rat: ⚠️ Depression alone does not negate capacity; bypassing a capable patient violates autonomy.

opts.text: Alternative — lithium and clonazepam were continued through the morning, and a routine pre-op lorazepam was given; the seizure was suboptimal and the treatment was ineffective
opts.rat: ⚠️ Anxiolytics and benzodiazepines/anticonvulsants/lithium suppress or distort the seizure. Demonstrates the medication-hold rule.

opts.text: Alternative — the patient was placed supine in recovery while still sedated; she aspirated and required suctioning
opts.rat: ⚠️ Side-lying protects the still-sedated airway. Demonstrates the recovery-position rule.

opts.text: Alternative — the nurse described ECT to the patient and family as 'shock therapy' that 'fries your brain'; the patient withdrew consent in distress
opts.rat: ⚠️ Stigmatizing language is harmful and inaccurate. Describe modern ECT correctly and dispel stigma.

opts.text: Alternative — dentures were left in place; the patient sustained dental injury during the treatment
opts.rat: ⚠️ Dentures must be removed before ECT; loose teeth identified. Demonstrates the pre-procedure dental/appliances rule.
